content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                    322-5901              322-6441           322-5444

January 20, 1978


John F. Hahn
County Counsel
Amador County
114 Main Street
Jackson, California 95642

Dear Mr. Hahn:

     This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation that took place
earlier this week.

     My understanding of the facts are as follows: There is currently
before the Board of Supervisors a motion to ask for a recommendation from
the Agricultural Advisory Committee as to whether the minimum acreage
requirements should be reduced from 40 to 20 acres for the purposes of
contracting under the Land Conservation Act of 1965. The question presented
is whether supervisors who have interest in lands subject to the Act may
participate in this vote.

     Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local govern-
ment shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt
to use his official position to influence a governmental
decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has
a financial interest.

     Section 87103(b) states:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on:

               ...
     (b)  Any real property in which the public official has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000);...

John F. Hahn
January 23, 1978
Page Two


     The supervisors must disqualify themselves if it is foreseeable that
the decision will materially affect their financial interest in a way that
is distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally.

     It is not foreseeable that the interests of the supervisors will be
affected materially by voting to ask for recommendations on the matter
currently before the Board. (It would be a speculative attempt to ascertain
what the recommendations of the Committee might be.) In addition, the
recommendations made by the Committee are not binding on the supervisors.
Accordingly, it is not readily foreseeable that the interests of the Board
might materially be affected by participating in the vote to send this
matter to the Committee for consideration.

     We will not address the issue raised in your letter dated December 2,
1977, at this time as to whether the board members must disqualify them-
selves from participating in the vote to reduce the acreage limitation.
In order to make a full and proper response, we will delay comment until
we have had an opportunity to analyze all the available facts. It is
possible that a report by the advisory committee as to impact of the proposed
acreage change upon the jurisdiction could be helpful in our analysis. If
a report from the Committee is forthcoming, we will delay our response to
your original opinion request until we have had an opportunity to analyze
the Committee's report as to the impact of the proposal, if approved, upon
the jurisdiction.

     If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact our office.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   [signature]

                                   Dwight Dickerson
                                   Legal Counsel
                                   Conflicts of Interest Division

DED:mfa

sections.question: Whether supervisors who have interest in lands subject to the Land Conservation Act of 1965 may participate in a vote on a motion to ask for a recommendation from the Agricultural Advisory Committee as to whether the minimum acreage requirements should be reduced from 40 to 20 acres for contracting purposes.
sections.conclusion: The supervisors must disqualify themselves if it is foreseeable that the decision will materially affect their financial interest in a way that is distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally. It is not foreseeable that the interests of the supervisors will be affected materially by voting to ask for recommendations on the matter currently before the Board. Accordingly, it is not readily foreseeable that the interests of the Board might materially be affected by participating in the vote to send this matter to the Committee for consideration.
sections.question_synthetic: May county supervisors with financial interests in land subject to the Land Conservation Act vote on a motion to request an Agricultural Advisory Committee recommendation regarding reduction of minimum acreage requirements from 40 to 20 acres?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Supervisors may participate in the vote to request the Agricultural Advisory Committee's recommendation because it is not reasonably foreseeable that this preliminary procedural vote will have a material financial effect on their interests, as the Committee's recommendations are non-binding and speculative. However, the FPPC declines to address whether supervisors must disqualify themselves from the subsequent vote to actually reduce the acreage limitation, pending receipt of the Committee's report on the proposal's impact.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION headers in document; question and conclusion extracted from body text. Letter confirms prior telephone conversation and partially defers full response pending additional information from advisory committee report. Section references to Political Reform Act sections 87100 and 87103(b) included in analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether supervisors who have interest in lands subject to the Land Conservation Act of 1965 may participate in a vote on a motion to ask for a recommendation from the Agricultural Advisory Committee as to whether the minimum acreage requirements should be reduced from 40 to 20 acres for contracting purposes.

CONCLUSION: The supervisors must disqualify themselves if it is foreseeable that the decision will materially affect their financial interest in a way that is distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally. It is not foreseeable that the interests of the supervisors will be affected materially by voting to ask for recommendations on the matter currently before the Board. Accordingly, it is not readily foreseeable that the interests of the Board might materially be affected by participating in the vote to send this matter to the Committee for consideration.
embedding.first_500_words: January 20, 1978 John F. Hahn County Counsel Amador County 114 Main Street Jackson, California 95642 Dear Mr. Hahn: This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation that took place earlier this week. My understanding of the facts are as follows: There is currently before the Board of Supervisors a motion to ask for a recommendation from the Agricultural Advisory Committee as to whether the minimum acreage requirements should be reduced from 40 to 20 acres for the purposes of contracting under the Land Conservation Act of 1965. The question presented is whether supervisors who have interest in lands subject to the Act may participate in this vote. Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act provides: No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Section 87103(b) states: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);... The supervisors must disqualify themselves if it is foreseeable that the decision will materially affect their financial interest in a way that is distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally. It is not foreseeable that the interests of the supervisors will be affected materially by voting to ask for recommendations on the matter currently before the Board. (It would be a speculative attempt to ascertain what the recommendations of the Committee might be.) In addition, the recommendations made by the Committee are not binding on the supervisors. Accordingly, it is not readily foreseeable that the interests of the Board might materially be affected by participating in the vote to send this matter to the Committee for consideration. We will not address the issue raised in your letter dated December 2, 1977, at this time as to whether the board members must disqualify themselves from participating in the vote to reduce the acreage limitation. In order to make a full and proper response, we will delay comment until we have had an opportunity to analyze all the available facts. It is possible that a report by the advisory committee as to impact of the proposed acreage change upon the jurisdiction could be helpful in our analysis. If a report from the Committee is forthcoming, we will delay our response to your original opinion request until we have had an opportunity to analyze the Committee's report as to the impact of the proposal, if approved, upon the jurisdiction. If you have any questions, please do not hesitate to contact our office. Sincerely, Dwight Dickerson Legal Counsel Conflicts of Interest Division
embedding.summary: In response to a query from Amador County Counsel regarding conflict of interest issues under the Political Reform Act, the FPPC advises that county supervisors with land interests subject to the Land Conservation Act may vote to refer a motion to the Agricultural Advisory Committee, but reserves judgment on whether they must disqualify themselves from a later vote on the actual acreage reduction pending analysis of the Committee's impact report.
